informal: Prove that the center of the product of two groups is the product of their centers.
lean:
theorem Artin_exercise_2_8_6 {G H : Type*} [Group G] [Group H] :
  Nonempty (center (G × H) ≃* (center G) × (center H))
reasoning: The informal claim "Z(G × H) = Z(G) × Z(H)" cannot be expressed as literal equality since Z(G × H) lives inside G × H while Z(G) × Z(H) is a product of subgroups; the standard faithful encoding is a group isomorphism, which is exactly `center (G × H) ≃* center G × center H`. Wrapping in `Nonempty` just asserts existence of such an iso (i.e. that the two groups are isomorphic), which matches the mathematical content of the statement. The hypotheses ([Group G], [Group H]) are exactly what the informal statement requires—no extra finiteness/commutativity/etc. assumptions, and G, H are universally quantified. Hence the Lean theorem captures the same claim, neither weaker nor stronger.
